title: Phoenix LiveView 1.2
blurb: Phoenix LiveView 1.2 was released, the latest version of the Elixir library for building real-time, interactive web UIs with server-rendered HTML and minimal client-side JavaScript. The update continues the framework's incremental hardening, refining its component and JS-hook APIs and tightening integration with the broader Phoenix stack while keeping the server-driven model that lets developers avoid writing a separate frontend.

title: Apple Foundation Models
blurb: Anthropic released Claude for Foundation Models, a Swift package that exposes Claude as a server-side language model inside Apple's Foundation Models framework. Apps drive it through the same `LanguageModelSession` API used for Apple's on-device model, switching to Claude by swapping the model argument when they need larger context, frontier reasoning, or server-side tools like web search and code execution. The beta targets the iOS 27 and macOS 27 betas, with requests billed at standard Anthropic API pricing.

title: Improvement in advanced Alzheimer’s disease following high-dose psilocybin
blurb: A case report describes cognitive and functional improvement in a patient with advanced Alzheimer's disease after treatment with high-dose psilocybin. The authors document gains in memory, mood, and daily functioning following the psychedelic compound, though a single uncontrolled case cannot establish efficacy and the findings will require controlled trials to validate.

title: Java's Project Valhalla finally lands a preview in JDK 28
blurb: Java's Project Valhalla reached its first preview in JDK 28, introducing value classes that combine the performance of primitives with the abstraction of objects. Architect Brian Goetz cautioned that the feature will likely remain in preview through the next long-term support release, continuing a development effort that has run for over a decade.

title: Scalable Circuit Learning for Interpreting Large Language Models
blurb: CircuitLasso uses sparse linear regression to learn interpretable SAE-feature circuits in LLMs at a fraction of intervention-based methods' compute cost.

title: Cisco fixes SD-WAN vManage flaw exploited in zero-day attacks
blurb: Cisco patched a Catalyst SD-WAN Manager flaw (CVE-2026-20262) exploited in zero-day attacks to escalate to root privileges.

title: Salesforce reels in customer support AI specialist Fin for $3.6B
blurb: Salesforce acquired customer support AI startup Fin for $3.6B, adding autonomous support agents that the company claims resolve three-quarters of customer queries without human intervention. The deal deepens Salesforce's push to embed AI agents across its service products, intensifying competition with Zendesk and Intercom for the enterprise customer-support automation market.

title: Banned Book Library in a Wi-Fi Smart Light Bulb
blurb: A developer hid a library of banned books inside the flash storage of a Wi-Fi smart light bulb, exploiting the spare memory on the cheap IoT device to serve the texts over a local web server. The project demonstrates how much general-purpose computing capacity sits unused in everyday connected hardware, and doubles as a low-profile method for distributing censored material.

title: A backdoor in a LinkedIn job offer
blurb: The author details a fake LinkedIn job offer whose take-home coding assignment hid a backdoor in an obfuscated config file, designed to execute attacker-controlled code the moment the candidate ran the project locally. The post walks through unpacking the payload and serves as a reminder that developers are now a direct target, since running unfamiliar repos on a work machine can quietly hand over credentials and access.

title: Copper transport drug restores memory and clears toxic Alzheimer's proteins
blurb: Monash University researchers reported that a copper-transport drug restored memory and cleared toxic amyloid and tau proteins in mouse models of Alzheimer's disease. The compound works by redistributing copper to brain regions where it is depleted, reactivating enzymes that break down the disease-linked proteins, though the results have yet to be replicated in human trials.

title: Anthropic’s Safety Superpower
blurb: Anthropic's conviction in its own safety commitment functions as a strategic asset, giving the company license to aggressively favor its business interests. The author argues this self-belief lets Anthropic justify commercially favorable moves and even challenge the U.S. government, framing decisions that serve its position as extensions of its safety mission.

title: Apple Foundation Models
blurb: Anthropic released Claude for Foundation Models, a Swift package that exposes Claude as a server-side language model inside Apple's Foundation Models framework. It conforms Claude to the framework's LanguageModel protocol, so developers use the same LanguageModelSession API for streaming, structured output, and tool calling, and can swap between Claude and Apple's on-device model per request on iOS, macOS, visionOS, and watchOS 27 betas.

title: Import AI 461: "Alignment is not on track"; FrontierCode; and synthetic research interns
blurb: The latest issue of Jack Clark's Import AI leads with the argument that AI alignment is not on track, contending that capability gains are outpacing the safety techniques meant to keep advanced systems controllable. It also covers FrontierCode, a benchmark for evaluating models on difficult coding tasks, and early experiments with synthetic research interns, AI agents tasked with running portions of the research process autonomously.

title: LiteLLM Vulnerability Chain Lets Low-Privilege Users Take Over AI Gateway Servers
blurb: Obsidian Security disclosed a vulnerability chain in LiteLLM that lets a default low-privilege account escalate to full admin and execute code on the server by combining three flaws. Because LiteLLM is a widely deployed open-source gateway brokering calls to more than 100 model providers, a takeover exposes every provider API key and secret the proxy holds.

title: Process-Oriented Evaluation of AI-Assisted Scientific Writing
blurb: This paper analyzes 869 keystroke-level edit logs and finds AI-generated abstracts gain sentence-level agency but lag human-authored ones on global coherence.

title: Sources: DeepSeek closed a $7.4B round at a $50B valuation under an unusual structure requiring investors to put capital into an LP run by CEO Liang Wenfeng (The Information)
blurb: DeepSeek closed a $7.4B round at a $50B valuation, its first outside funding, under a structure that requires investors to route capital through a limited partnership controlled by CEO Liang Wenfeng. The arrangement, which raised more than 50 billion yuan, keeps Liang in direct control of how the new capital is deployed across the Chinese AI lab.

title: Agent trajectories as programs: fingerprinting and programming coding-agent behavior
blurb: This paper introduces a method for fingerprinting coding agents by their procedural behavior rather than their benchmark scores, using a compressive vocabulary induction technique that captures how an agent solves problems. Across ten agents, a probe attributes an unseen trajectory to the correct agent with 85.7% accuracy, and an analysis of SWE-Bench finds that behavior is most similar between models from comparable release periods.

title: Sources: Trump officials weighed Anthropic export controls weeks before forcing its models offline, after a dispute over Mythos access for a China-linked firm (Washington Post)
blurb: Trump administration officials weighed export controls on Anthropic for weeks before forcing its AI models offline, following a dispute over a China-linked firm's access to Anthropic's Mythos. The Washington Post reported the controls were considered after the company restricted that firm's access, escalating tensions between the lab and the administration over how AI models reach foreign entities.

title: Dynamic Malicious Skills in Agentic AI
blurb: This paper demonstrates dynamic malicious skills, an attack where instructions embedded in natural-language skill documentation like SKILL.md induce an agent to inject malicious logic into an otherwise benign skill at runtime. Tested against OpenHands and Claude Code, the attack succeeded at non-trivial rates, and the authors propose a defense using OS kernel-enforced read-only mounts that blocks the modifications while preserving benign skill functionality.

title: Claude Corps
blurb: Anthropic launched Claude Corps, a program pairing its engineers with organizations to build and deploy Claude-powered tools.

title: The hidden pattern behind successful products | Mark Pincus (founder of Zynga)
blurb: Successful products almost never come from a genuinely new idea. Mark Pincus argues your instincts about what to build are usually right, but the specific idea is usually wrong, so the move is to start from something already proven and make it meaningfully better before reaching for something new. His "Proven, Better, New" framework puts the odds in that order: take a validated category, find the dimension where you can win, and treat pure novelty as the riskiest bet rather than the goal.

title: SpaceX is public: Everything you need to know post-IPO
blurb: SpaceX completed its IPO, and the S-1 finally puts numbers behind a company that has spent two decades as a private black box. The filing reveals the real split between the launch business and Starlink, who cashed in on pre-IPO secondary deals, and which early backers stand to win or lose now that the shares trade publicly. For founders, the bigger signal is that the most capital-intensive hard-tech bet of the era reached the public markets, which reopens a path that software-era venture had all but written off.

title: How to earn a billion dollars
blurb: The reliable way to earn a billion dollars is to start a company that grows, because wealth comes from owning a large share of something that scales, not from being paid for your time. Salary caps what you can make, while equity in a fast-growing startup compounds without a ceiling. The essay's point for founders is that the size of the outcome tracks the value you create multiplied by how much of it you keep.

title: SpaceX’s biggest-ever IPO just grew to $85.7 billion raised
blurb: SpaceX's IPO grew to $85.7 billion raised after underwriters maxed out their share purchases, making it the largest public offering on record. The buy-up adds to an already historic total, pushing demand for the launch and Starlink business well past initial expectations. A raise of this size resets the ceiling for what a private company can pull from public markets, and signals that investor appetite for hard-tech and space infrastructure now rivals the demand once reserved for software.

title: Not everyone is using AI for everything
blurb: Most people who try AI use it occasionally, and a large share of the population isn't using it at all. Adoption has barely moved over the past year, despite the assumption that AI is now woven into everyone's daily work. The notable shift is in sentiment, with negative feelings about AI climbing as many people cite real concerns and see little perceived value in it.

title: Kuala Lumpur-based Respond.io, which uses AI agents to manage customer conversations, raised a $62.5M Series B led by Camber, following a $7M Series A in 2022 (Kate Park/TechCrunch)
blurb: Respond.io raised a $62.5M Series B led by Camber to run customer conversations across messaging apps with AI agents.

title: OpenRouter debuts Fusion, a tool that prompts multiple AI models in parallel, claiming it can "reach and surpass Fable-level performance on deep research tasks" (Brian Thomas/OpenRouter Blog)
blurb: OpenRouter launched Fusion, which runs several models in parallel on a research task, then has a judge model extract structure and a synthesizer write the final answer grounded in their combined output. OpenRouter claims this panel approach reaches and surpasses Fable-level performance on deep research tasks, because synthesizing multiple models consistently beats what any single model produces. Beyond-frontier results become achievable from frontier panels without paying frontier prices per query.

title: Formal methods and the future of programming
blurb: Jane Street details how it applies formal methods across its codebase, using techniques like type-driven design, property-based testing, and verification tools to catch bugs that conventional testing misses. The series argues that lightweight formal methods are now practical for everyday production code rather than confined to academia or safety-critical systems, with OCaml's type system doing much of the heavy lifting.

title: Linux 7.1
blurb: Linus Torvalds released Linux 7.1, the latest mainline kernel, following the standard two-week stabilization period after the final release candidate. As with past major-number bumps, the jump carries no special significance beyond keeping minor version numbers from growing unwieldy, with the bulk of the changes landing as routine driver updates, filesystem fixes, and incremental subsystem improvements.

title: Phoenix LiveView 1.2
blurb: Phoenix LiveView 1.2 introduces colocated hooks and colocated JS, which let developers write client-side JavaScript directly inside HEEx templates instead of maintaining separate hook files, with the JS extracted at compile time and bundled by the asset pipeline. The release also adds a new portal API for rendering content outside the current DOM tree, useful for modals and tooltips, and ships compatibility fixes for the latest Phoenix and Elixir versions.

title: Arch Linux locks down AUR signups amid wave of malicious commits
blurb: Arch Linux froze new account signups on the Arch User Repository after attackers flooded it with malicious commits that poisoned package updates. The community-maintained AUR ships build scripts rather than vetted binaries, so the lockdown buys maintainers time to clean compromised packages and review the backlog before reopening registration to contributors.

title: TokenPilot: Cache-Efficient Context Management for LLM Agents
blurb: TokenPilot manages LLM agent context to stay cache-friendly, cutting inference costs 61% and 56% on two benchmarks without breaking prompt prefix continuity.

title: Banned Book Library in a Wi-Fi Smart Light Bulb
blurb: A developer flashed a Wi-Fi smart bulb's ESP8266 microcontroller to host a hidden library of banned books served over a local web page. The post walks through dumping the stock firmware, repurposing the chip's flash storage to hold the text, and serving it via a captive portal that anyone nearby can connect to without internet access.

title: How memory safety CVEs differ between Rust and C/C++
blurb: The author analyzes memory safety CVE data and argues that Rust's guarantees shift the nature of vulnerabilities rather than just reducing their count, since unsafe blocks and logic errors still produce memory bugs that look different from C/C++ classics. The post contends that Rust's compiler eliminates most use-after-free and buffer-overflow patterns up front, concentrating remaining issues in a smaller, auditable surface where reviewers can focus their attention.

title: Configuration Smells in AGENTS.md Files: Common Mistakes in Configuring Coding Agents
blurb: This paper presents the first catalog of "configuration smells" in AGENTS.md and CLAUDE.md files, the instruction files that steer coding agents on architecture, conventions, and testing. After mining repositories and reviewing 100 popular open-source projects, the authors define six smells with automated detection heuristics, finding Lint Leakage (linter rules duplicated into agent instructions) the most common at 62% of files.

title: North Korean Hackers Are Turning Developer Tools Into Malware Delivery Channels
blurb: Proofpoint flagged two phishing campaigns from the North Korean threat cluster Contagious Interview, which lures developers with fake recruiter outreach and code review requests before delivering malware through trusted dev tooling. The activity overlaps with the group also tracked as Famous Chollima and Void Dokkaebi, signaling that job-application and code-review workflows are now active attack surfaces engineers should treat with the same suspicion as unsolicited email attachments.

title: Dynamic Malicious Skills in Agentic AI
blurb: Malicious instructions hidden in SKILL.md docs can make agents like Claude Code inject harmful logic at runtime; kernel read-only mounts block it.

title: What’s new in data agents: Supercharging your AI workflows
blurb: Google introduced new data agents across its Agentic Data Cloud, including agents in Conversational Analytics for business analysts and tooling for data scientists and engineers to build agents that ground reasoning in live enterprise data. The pitch is a deterministic, template-driven developer framework with unified governance, aiming for near-100% query accuracy by giving agents context across both operational and analytical systems.

title: Free SQL→ER diagram tool, runs in the browser, nothing uploaded
blurb: A free browser-based tool converts SQL schema definitions into entity-relationship diagrams, with all parsing done client-side so nothing is uploaded to a server. The local-only approach lets data teams diagram production schemas without sending DDL to a third party, and the tool reads standard CREATE TABLE statements to map tables, columns, and foreign-key relationships.

title: Directory-Aware Query and Maintenance in Vector Databases
blurb: Vector databases store metadata as flat scalar attributes, forcing directory-scoped retrieval over code repos, enterprise docs, and agent memories into expensive application-layer workarounds. This paper treats directory hierarchy as a first-class capability, formalizing two operators: Directory-Semantic Query for scoped retrieval and Directory-Semantic Maintenance for structural updates. It compares query-time and ingestion-time path expansion against a Trie-based Hierarchical Index, showing that flattening the hierarchy makes recursive scope resolution costly while the trie keeps both queries and updates consistent.

title: When Does q-error Predict Plan Regret? Three Regimes of Cardinality-Estimation Error
blurb: This paper challenges the field's reliance on q-error as a proxy for query-plan quality, offering a measurement-driven account of when it holds. The authors identify three regimes: for small estimation errors, a true-point condition number predicts plan regret and beats q-error; for the large errors where learned estimators actually operate, an estimator-independent ACS-infinity measure flags regret-prone queries (Spearman rho ~0.54 on STATS-CEB) while q-error is nearly useless at the query level (rho ~0.05).

title: Fluent AI, Liquid Glass, flaw as a feature, AX Design
blurb: A weekly designer reading list led by an essay arguing AI-generated design fails not by being ugly but by being fluent.

title: What’s !important #13: @function, alpha(), CSS Wordle, and More
blurb: The latest issue of CSS-Tricks' What's !important roundup covers the new `@function` rule for authoring custom CSS functions, the `alpha()` function for adjusting opacity on existing colors, and Grid Lanes for more flexible track placement. It also surfaces lesser-known `dialog` element behaviors and a CSS-only Wordle build worth studying for layout tricks.

title: Typst 0.15.0
blurb: Typst released version 0.15.0 of its markup-based typesetting system, the open-source alternative to LaTeX that compiles plain-text source into print-ready PDFs with a scripting model designers can actually read. The release continues work on HTML export and PDF accessibility tagging, extending Typst beyond academic papers toward the styled reports, specs, and documentation that design teams produce alongside their interface work.

title: The autonomy dial: a pattern toolkit for designing human control over AI
blurb: This essay introduces the "autonomy dial," a framework for calibrating how much an AI system acts independently versus deferring to a person, paired with six reusable patterns for human oversight. It is a useful vocabulary for design teams shipping AI features who keep relitigating the same control trade-offs without a shared way to name the levels in between full automation and full manual.

title: Evolution & Foundation: AI Shares Creative Control
blurb: A research framework pairs genetic algorithms with multimodal AI aesthetic judgment to evolve 3D organic forms, shifting the artist's role from direct curation to system design.

title: LiteLLM Vulnerability Chain Lets Low-Privilege Users Take Over AI Gateway Servers
blurb: Researchers at Obsidian Security disclosed a chain of three vulnerabilities in LiteLLM, the open-source AI gateway brokering calls to over 100 model providers, that lets a default low-privilege account escalate to full admin and achieve remote code execution on the proxy server. A server takeover exposes every stored provider API key, so operators should patch immediately, audit account privileges, and rotate all credentials held by the gateway.

title: Palo Alto Warns of Active Exploitation of PAN-OS GlobalProtect VPN Flaw
blurb: Palo Alto Networks confirmed active exploitation of CVE-2026-0257 (CVSS 7.8), an authentication-bypass flaw in the portal and gateway components of PAN-OS, by an unknown threat actor gaining unauthorized access to GlobalProtect portals. Organizations running affected PAN-OS versions on internet-facing GlobalProtect should patch immediately, audit portal access logs for unauthorized sessions, and rotate credentials on appliances that may already be compromised.

title: The FCC Wants to Eliminate Burner Phones
blurb: The FCC proposed a rule requiring US telecoms to collect and store personal data on nearly all phone customers, including a government-issued ID number and physical address, effectively eliminating burner phones whose accounts aren't tied to an identity. The agency frames the mandate as an anti-scam measure, but privacy advocates warn it builds a centralized trove of identity data with significant surveillance and cybersecurity knock-on risks.

title: obliv-clang: Real-World Oblivious Programming in C++
blurb: This paper introduces obliv-clang, a compilation-time tool that checks the obliviousness of C++ programs to defend against timing and access-pattern side-channel attacks on confidential data in trusted execution environments. It supports rich C++ features including arbitrarily nested pointers, formally proves the soundness of its checking rules, and aims to integrate with existing industry codebases while keeping compilation overhead and runtime performance costs low.

title: Snyk VulnBench JS 1.0: Can LLMs Find the Same Bugs Twice?
blurb: This paper introduces VulnBench JS 1.0, a benchmark that ran 300 repeated scans to measure how repeatable agentic LLM security review is on identical JavaScript code, prompts, and harness. Reference-matched findings were stable (134 of 158 appeared in all five repetitions), but unmatched extra findings varied heavily, with 80 of 161 surfacing in just one of five runs, suggesting agentic SAST should complement, not replace, deterministic static analysis.

title: DOJ seizes CFAKE, SOCFAKE deepfake nude sites under TAKE IT DOWN Act
blurb: The DOJ seized the CFAKE.com and SOCFAKE.com domains, which allegedly hosted nonconsensual AI-generated nude images and videos of women, in what appears to be the first publicly announced domain seizure under the TAKE IT DOWN Act. The action signals that the DOJ will use the new law to pursue infrastructure behind deepfake abuse sites, setting an enforcement precedent for operators hosting nonconsensual synthetic imagery.

title: Transferable Self-Evolving Playbooks for Agentic Security Auditing
blurb: EvoHunt automates audit playbook creation for LLM security agents, replacing human-curated prompts and heuristics with a closed evolution loop: an audit agent runs the playbook, an evaluator scores findings against ground truth, and a reviser commits updates. The authors show evolved playbooks transfer the audit procedure to weaker agents, raising their vulnerability-discovery capability without retraining the underlying model. For defenders, this points toward scalable agentic auditing where procedural knowledge, not just model strength, drives results.

title: Di5Guise: 5G Privacy with vSIM
blurb: This paper introduces Di5Guise, a 5G privacy architecture that decouples device identity from subscriber identity to stop network-level correlation attacks. Current eSIMs bind every provisioned subscriber profile to a fixed device profile (secret key, certificate, and eUICC ID), letting an operator-level adversary tie rotating identities back to one device and reconstruct a full pattern of life. Its vSIM design breaks that binding at the root, so identity rotation and traffic obfuscation actually hold.

title: Banned Book Library in a Wi-Fi Smart Light Bulb
blurb: A researcher hid a library of banned books inside the flash memory of a Wi-Fi smart light bulb.

title: Scalable Malware Family Classification Using Quantum Kernel Based Machine Learning
blurb: A quantum kernel ML framework classifies malware families at scale, using LDA projection and fidelity-based quantum circuits for accuracy.

title: One-Click Microsoft 365 Copilot Flaw Could Have Let Attackers Steal Emails, Files, and MFA Codes
blurb: Varonis Threat Labs disclosed SearchLeak, a now-patched flaw in Microsoft 365 Copilot Enterprise Search that chained three bugs into a one-click path for exfiltrating emails, calendar data, indexed files, and MFA codes. Because the malicious link resolved to a legitimate microsoft.com domain, conventional anti-phishing and URL-filtering tools failed to flag it, leaving enterprises reliant on Microsoft's server-side fix rather than their own controls.

title: Salesforce reels in customer support AI specialist Fin for $3.6B
blurb: Salesforce acquired customer-support AI vendor Fin for $3.6B, adding agents the company claims resolve roughly 75% of customer queries without human intervention. The deal strengthens Salesforce's Agentforce push into autonomous service automation and pressures rivals like ServiceNow and Zendesk competing for the same enterprise support-deflection budgets.

title: Introducing the OpenAI Partner Network
blurb: OpenAI launched the Partner Network and committed $150M to help consultancies and systems integrators deploy enterprise AI for their clients. The program gives partners co-selling support, technical resources, and go-to-market funding, positioning OpenAI against the established Microsoft, Accenture, and Deloitte channel relationships that enterprises already lean on for AI rollouts.

title: FBI disrupts massive AI-powered phishing service using a million URLs
blurb: The FBI, in coordination with Google and Black Lotus Labs, dismantled a Chinese phishing-as-a-service operation called Outsider Enterprise that ran thousands of sites across roughly a million URLs to steal credit card data and passwords. The takedown removes infrastructure that lowered the technical bar for credential theft, raising the baseline of AI-assisted phishing that enterprise security teams now have to defend against.

title: Midsize companies expect AI gains, but integration woes curb progress
blurb: Midsize firms expect AI productivity gains, but security, data readiness, and integration gaps continue to slow broader adoption.

title: What’s new in data agents: Supercharging your AI workflows
blurb: Google added new data agents across its Agentic Data Cloud, including agents in Conversational Analytics for business analysts and tooling for data scientists, engineers, and database teams to ground reasoning in real-time enterprise data. The release targets the accuracy and access-control gaps that block generic AI platforms from enterprise databases, pairing template-driven development with unified governance across operational and analytical systems.

title: Measurement Study of Post-Quantum Readiness of Internet: 2026
blurb: A measurement study of 32,011 domains finds that 49.3% now support hybrid post-quantum key exchange like MLKEM768 with X25519, but 15.70% still run TLS 1.2, concentrated in banking and government. The gap matters for IT leaders mapping crypto-agility timelines, since the laggards sit in exactly the regulated sectors most exposed to "harvest now, decrypt later" attacks and most expensive to remediate.

title: France's digital sovereignty push is struggling to escape the Microsoft gravity well
blurb: France's digital sovereignty effort is hitting a wall on the application layer, where a Nextcloud rollout has migrated storage off Microsoft but failed to move users off Office. The lesson for IT leaders is that swapping infrastructure is the easy part; replacing entrenched productivity software means fighting user habit, file-format lock-in, and feature parity gaps that open-source alternatives still struggle to close.

title: LiteLLM Vulnerability Chain Lets Low-Privilege Users Take Over AI Gateway Servers
blurb: Researchers at Obsidian Security disclosed a chain of three vulnerabilities in LiteLLM that lets a default low-privilege account escalate to full admin and execute code on the proxy server. Because LiteLLM brokers calls to more than 100 model providers behind one OpenAI-compatible interface, a takeover exposes every provider API key it holds, making the gateway a single point of failure worth patching immediately.

title: Improving Arm64 support in CNCF projects with OCI credits
blurb: The CNCF detailed how it's using Oracle Cloud Infrastructure credits to expand Arm64 build and test coverage across its projects, addressing a gap as Arm instances now make up over 50% of new AWS launches and a third on Azure. The credits give maintainers native Arm64 CI runners, removing the emulation overhead and cross-compilation toil that previously slowed multi-arch release pipelines.

title: AI demands more engineering discipline. Not less (xpost)
blurb: Charity Majors argues that AI-assisted development raises the bar for engineering discipline rather than lowering it, drawing a parallel to the shift from hand-maintained server pets to immutable infrastructure. The piece contends that generating more code faster makes review, testing, observability, and ownership boundaries more important, since teams now ship volume they did not personally write and must still operate in production.

title: Linux 7.1
blurb: Linus Torvalds announced the Linux 7.1 kernel on the LKML, closing the merge window with the release now tagged stable and available from kernel.org. The cycle continues the steady cadence of incremental driver, filesystem, and scheduler updates that platform teams fold into their base images, with distro and managed-node rollouts to follow over the coming weeks.

title: AWS WAF adds AI traffic monetization capability to help content owners charge AI bots for content access
blurb: AWS WAF added AI traffic monetization, a Bot Control capability that lets content providers and publishers price, meter, and collect payment from AI bots and agents hitting their content and APIs. Owners set a price, accept payment through third-party providers, and grant scoped access directly at the edge, turning unwanted crawler traffic into a revenue stream without standing up separate gateway infrastructure.

title: What job interviews taught me about Kubernetes
blurb: The author uses Kubernetes interview questions as a lens for the internals practitioners gloss over in daily operations, walking through how the scheduler, kubelet, and control loops actually behave under questioning. The piece argues that interview prep exposes the gap between running kubectl commands and understanding why a pod is stuck pending or a node goes NotReady. Useful as a self-audit for platform engineers who operate clusters without having traced the reconciliation path end to end.

title: OptinMonster WordPress plugin hacked in CDN supply-chain attack
blurb: Three Awesome Motive WordPress plugins, OptinMonster, TrustPulse, and PushEngage, were compromised in a supply-chain attack on the company's CDN, exposing sites that rely on them for email capture, social proof, and push notifications. Marketing teams running these tools should audit installs, rotate API keys, and review CDN-loaded scripts, since the affected plugins sit directly in the lead-capture and conversion stack.

title: Meta’s new ‘AI Mode’ on Facebook pulls from public info across its platforms
blurb: Meta is rolling out an "AI Mode" on Facebook that pulls from public information across its platforms to power conversational search and recommendations, part of a broader push to lift engagement and close the AI gap with rivals. For marketers, it signals another surface where AI-mediated answers sit between users and brand content, making public-facing Facebook and Instagram presence a feed for Meta's models rather than a direct channel to the audience.

title: Meta’s new ‘AI Mode’ on Facebook pulls from public info across its platforms
blurb: Meta began rolling out a suite of AI features on Facebook, including an "AI Mode" that draws on public information across its platforms to answer questions and surface content. The move is Meta's bid to close the gap with rivals in the AI race while keeping users engaged longer, signaling that assistant-style discovery is becoming table stakes inside consumer social products.

title: How difficult could it be to design a chatbot?
blurb: A study on blood-donation chatbots found culture-tailored framing can backfire: leading with personal benefit undermined the altruistic motivation to donate.

title: Process-Oriented Evaluation of AI-Assisted Scientific Writing
blurb: A study of 869 keystroke-level edit logs found people restructure human abstracts but switch to word-swapping once they learn AI wrote the draft.

title: Beyond Usability: A UX Case Study on Using "Withdrawal Design" to Challenge Engagement Metrics in Social Robotics
blurb: A UX case study on social robots for autistic children argues that high engagement and usability scores can mask ecological harm. In an 8-week home RCT (N=40), keeping a consumer robot lowered anxiety but was tied to weaker gains in social motivation and emotion recognition, while withdrawal sometimes pushed children toward human interaction. The takeaway for PMs: "engagement" is an output metric that can siphon behavior into the product itself, so success should be judged by outcomes beyond the product, not stickiness alone.

title: The autonomy dial: a pattern toolkit for designing human control over AI
blurb: This essay offers a "autonomy dial" framework for deciding how much an AI feature acts on its own, paired with six reusable patterns for keeping humans in control. For PMs shipping AI features, it turns a vague "how much should we automate" debate into a concrete spec decision, with the control patterns serving as a checklist for where oversight, confirmation, or reversibility need to be designed in.

title: UXBench: Measuring the Actionability of LLM-Generated UX Critiques
blurb: UXBench introduces a benchmark that evaluates how well frontier LLMs perform as UX judges, scoring their interface critiques across seven rubric dimensions and forcing models to collect browser interaction evidence before reporting problems. Critique quality is graded by whether a downstream repair agent can actually improve the interface, giving PMs a concrete signal on which models produce actionable usability feedback rather than plausible-sounding notes.

title: Sui Processes $65 Billion in Stablecoin Transfers in Five Days After Zeroing Out Fees
blurb: Sui processed nearly $65B in stablecoin transfers in the five days since June 10, when it removed gas fees on stablecoin transactions to position the Layer 1 as a fee-free settlement rail. The gasless design targets institutional and agentic payment flows, where per-transaction costs otherwise compound across high-volume automated transfers.

title: Kraken launches crypto perps trading in the US on Kraken Pro
blurb: Kraken launched perpetual futures trading for US customers on Kraken Pro, offering crypto perps through the CFTC-regulated infrastructure it gained by acquiring Bitnomial, a licensed exchange, clearinghouse, and brokerage, in May. The move gives Kraken a domestic, fully regulated venue for leveraged derivatives, a product US traders previously accessed mainly through offshore platforms.

title: Aztec Connect Drained of $2.1M Through Deprecated Contract Three Years After Shutdown
blurb: An attacker exploited a proof-verification flaw in Aztec Connect's deprecated RollupProcessorV3 contract to withdraw roughly $2.1M, including 909 ETH, from a privacy bridge that Aztec Labs shut down three years ago. Aztec no longer controls the abandoned contract and could not intervene, underscoring the lingering risk of leaving immutable code deployed on-chain after a protocol winds down.

title: did:crdt: Coordination-Free Decentralised Identifiers via Signed CRDTs
blurb: This paper introduces did:crdt, a Decentralised Identifier method that updates DID documents without any ledger, sequencer, or global ordering, composing each document from signed Conflict-Free Replicated Data Types that merge deterministically on concurrent edits. The design eliminates the fees, latency, and consensus requirements of blockchain-anchored and Sidetree methods, needing only causal delivery of signed deltas to keep replicas convergent.

title: Bitcoin Policy UK CEO calls Michael Saylor’s STRC investment promotion ‘dishonest’
blurb: Bitcoin Policy UK CEO Susie Ward called Michael Saylor's promotion of Strategy's STRC preferred stock "dishonest," saying his video on the instrument's yield implied the investment carried no risk. The criticism targets how Strategy markets STRC to retail buyers, a vehicle Saylor has used to fund the company's continued bitcoin accumulation.

title: Kraken and Coinbase Bring Perps Onshore as US Derivatives Markets Shift
blurb: Kraken and Coinbase each launched perpetual futures products on Monday, with Kraken going live on CFTC-regulated crypto perps through Bitnomial and Coinbase rolling out four thematic equity-index perpetuals on US soil. The moves bring a market structure that has lived offshore onto regulated American venues, opening domestic access to leveraged derivatives that previously drove traders to platforms like Binance and Bybit.

title: Robust and Automated Reconfiguration of Byzantine Wide-Area Replication
blurb: This paper identifies three vulnerabilities in latency-based reconfiguration of BFT consensus, where replicas dynamically assign leaders and tune voting weights based on network monitoring. The authors propose Beware, a framework that filters falsified latency reports, computes robust weight distributions, and uses machine learning to converge on Byzantine-resilient configurations. The work matters for blockchain validator sets that optimize throughput through adaptive configuration, since unguarded tuning hands adversarial nodes a new attack surface.

title: The Ghosts of Polymarket: When Off-Chain Matches Meet On-Chain Reverts
blurb: This paper analyzes "Ghost Fills" on Polymarket, the consistency gap created by its hybrid architecture that matches orders off-chain but settles them on-chain. The authors built GHOSTHUNTER to reconstruct failed settlements from on-chain traces, examining 1,952,440 reverted match-order transactions where attackers exploited the time gap to invalidate already-matched orders before finalization. They identify four attack vectors—nonce bump, balance drain, allowance revoke, and proxy trap—realized across 35 evolving variants.

title: Beyond the Smile: A Hybrid Convolutional VAE for Crypto Volatility Surfaces
blurb: A convolutional VAE reconstructs BTC and ETH implied-volatility surfaces, cutting completion error eightfold versus a quadratic smile re-fit at 50% masking.

title: Sui Processes $65 Billion in Stablecoin Transfers in Five Days After Zeroing Out Fees
blurb: Sui processed nearly $65B in stablecoin transfers since June 10 after zeroing out gas fees for stablecoin movement, the Layer 1's gasless design absorbing transaction costs at the protocol level. The shift recasts Sui as a fee-free settlement rail aimed at institutional and agentic payments, where per-transaction cost has been a barrier to high-volume, machine-initiated flows.

title: Kraken launches crypto perps trading in the US on Kraken Pro
blurb: Kraken launched perpetual futures trading for US customers on Kraken Pro, enabled by its May acquisition of Bitnomial, a CFTC-licensed exchange, clearinghouse, and brokerage. The launch gives Kraken a regulated path to offer crypto perps domestically, a product US traders previously accessed mostly through offshore venues operating outside CFTC oversight.

title: Bernstein says Robinhood could see ‘strong tailwinds’ as World Cup drives record prediction market volumes
blurb: Bernstein projects Robinhood's prediction market revenue will climb from $150M in 2025 to $586M in 2026, citing record event-contract volumes driven by the World Cup. The analysts see strong tailwinds for the category as Robinhood expands beyond equities and crypto into prediction markets, a segment now contributing materially to its trading revenue mix.

title: Bitcoin tops $65,000 as reports of US-Iran peace deal ease geopolitical fears
blurb: Bitcoin topped $65,000 as reports of a US-Iran peace deal eased geopolitical fears and lifted risk sentiment.

title: Kraken and Coinbase Bring Perps Onshore as US Derivatives Markets Shift
blurb: Kraken and Coinbase each launched perpetual futures products Monday, with Kraken going live on CFTC-regulated crypto contracts through Bitnomial and Coinbase introducing four thematic equity-index perpetuals on US soil. The moves bring a product long confined to offshore venues under domestic regulatory oversight, intensifying competition for US derivatives volume as the CFTC signals openness to onshore crypto perps.

title: Invisible Manipulation Channels in AI-Assisted Financial Advisory: Implications for Market Integrity and Regulatory Design
blurb: This paper identifies a manipulation channel at the sampling layer of LLM inference that lets adversaries systematically bias AI-generated credit and investment opinions while passing output-based audits, including statistical watermarking. The authors show detection is statistically hard, since the KL divergence between manipulated and normal output distributions can be driven arbitrarily small, forcing impractically large sample sizes. For regulators relying on output audits to police AI advisory, the implication is that integrity controls must move into the inference pipeline itself.
